title: RFP: SLA & Measurement Methodology Clarifications (Section 3)
preview:
From: Laura Chen <laura.chen@finplus.com>
To: karthik.iyer@redwood.com
Cc: rafael.mendes@redwood.com
Date: Sat, 14 Feb 2026 08:12:00 -0800
Subject: RFP: SLA & Measurement Methodology Clarifications (Section 3)

Hi Karthik,

Thanks for submitting Redwood's RFP responses. Our procurement and SRE teams reviewed Section 3 (SLA/SLO). We have a few clarifications before we advance to legal:

1) Uptime commitment: You listed 99.95% monthly availability for the hosted inference API. Please confirm whether that is measured at the HTTP 200 success rate at the edge, or at the backend GPU serving layer.
2) Latency SLOs: For the 95th/99th percentile latency targets you provided, what's the sampling window and traffic weighting? Do you exclude warmups or preflight checks?
3) Measurement methodology: Provide the formula/aggregation method you will use to calculate downtime and p95/p99 (e.g., rolling window, calendar month, what constitutes an outage incident).
4) Service credits: How are credits calculated? Are credits capped per month and per incident?
5) Scheduled maintenance: Define notification windows and whether maintenance is excluded from uptime calculation.

Could you respond with our requested detail as a single document (or spreadsheet) we can attach to the contract? Our target is to resolve these items by EOD Monday so legal can redline the Master Services Agreement on Tuesday.

Appreciate the quick turnaround — feel free to call Aaron (cc'd) for any urgent clarifications.

Regards,
Laura Chen
Senior Procurement Manager
FinPlus Financial Systems

--
Laura Chen
FinPlus Financial Systems
laura.chen@finplus.com
+1 (415) 555-1298


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Laura Chen <laura.chen@finplus.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 14 Feb 2026 13:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: RFP: SLA & Measurement Methodology Clarifications (Section 3)

Laura —

Thanks for the clear list. We've pulled together a consolidated response and an attached workbook that details our measurement formulas and sample calculations. High level answers below; detailed definitions are in the attached PDF and the spreadsheet (see attachments).

1) Uptime measurement: We measure availability at the API gateway edge (HTTP-level) after retries. This reflects customer-observed successful completions. Backend serving-layer losses (e.g., a single GPU host failure masked by retry/fallback) are visible in our telemetry but availability is computed at the edge. See attachment Redwood-SLA-Response-20260214.pdf, Section 1.

2) Latency SLOs: Percentiles are computed on successful request completion times measured at the ingress gateway. We calculate p95 and p99 over a rolling 30-day calendar window aggregated per calendar month for reporting. Requests that fail (non-2xx) are excluded from p95/p99 latency calculations but are counted toward availability. We exclude synthetic warmup traffic from production sampling via request tags; those requests are filtered out before percentile aggregation.

3) Measurement methodology: Downtime is defined as any minute in which the edge availability drops below 99% after automatic retries defined in our client retry policy. Monthly availability = (Total minutes in calendar month - outage minutes) / Total minutes in month. P95/P99 use the joined sample of all successful user requests, weighted equally (no per-customer weighting). See spreadsheet tab "Formulas" for exact SQL-like pseudo-code and example calculations.

4) Service credits: Credits are calculated as a percentage of monthly invoice for Redwood-hosted inference, per the following bands: 99.95-100% = 0% credit; 99.9-99.949% = 10% credit; 99.5-99.899% = 25% credit; <99.5% = 50% credit. Credits are capped at 50% per calendar month and apply only to Redwood-hosted plan usage charges. For multi-tenant incidents affecting multiple customers, credits are prorated by affected usage minutes. Single-customer incidents where the customer has Dedicated capacity are handled under Dedicated terms (not this credit table).

5) Scheduled maintenance: Planned maintenance windows of up to 4 hours per quarter are excluded from uptime calculations when we provide >72 hours advance notice via the Console and the scheduled maintenance is published to the customer's SLA notifications. Emergency maintenance is not excluded.

Next steps: Attached are (1) Redwood-SLA-Response-20260214.pdf with definitions and sample SQL pseudo-code, and (2) SLA-Measurement-Methodology.xlsx with worked examples. We're happy to take this into contract as an exhibit. If you want, Rafael and I can run a 30-minute SRE walkthrough with your team Monday morning to walk through the sample calculations.

Best,

Karthik Iyer
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
karthik.iyer@redwood.com
+1 (650) 555-0199

Attachments: Redwood-SLA-Response-20260214.pdf (application/pdf), SLA-Measurement-Methodology.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet)


From: Aaron Miles <aaron.miles@finplus.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Laura Chen <laura.chen@finplus.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2026 09:03:00 -0800
Subject: Re: RFP: SLA & Measurement Methodology Clarifications (Section 3)

Karthik —

Thanks for the quick reply and attachments. A couple of follow-ups from our SRE:

- When you say "after retries" for availability, what retry window and backoff do you apply by default? The SRE team needs the default policy values to evaluate user-perceived availability.
- For the credit proration on multi-tenant incidents, can you provide an example calculation (customer A had 70% of affected minutes, customer B 30% -> how credits are allocated)?
- For the p99 sampling, are you using request start-to-end or service processing time (i.e., excluding network egress to client)?

Also — we need a machine-readable report once per month (CSV or XLSX) with the raw minute-level availability series and computed monthly metrics so our monitoring team can independently verify. Can you commit to delivering that within 5 business days of month close?

Appreciate it — we need these answers for legal to finalize exhibit A.

Aaron
Director, SRE Programs
FinPlus


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Aaron Miles <aaron.miles@finplus.com>
Cc: Laura Chen <laura.chen@finplus.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 16 Feb 2026 16:40:00 -0800
Subject: Re: RFP: SLA & Measurement Methodology Clarifications (Section 3)

Aaron —

Good questions. Short answers first, then details below.

- Default retry policy: Client SDKs and gateway employ up to 3 attempts with exponential backoff starting at 100ms, doubling each attempt (100ms, 200ms, 400ms). Retries are only for idempotent requests; non-idempotent requests are not retried by default. We include retries when computing availability to reflect a user experience that benefits from short automatic retries.

- Credit proration example: If a multi-tenant incident affected 1000 total affected usage-minutes and Customer A accounted for 700 minutes and Customer B 300 minutes, credits are allocated proportionally by affected usage. Example: total eligible credit = $10,000 -> A receives $7,000 credit, B receives $3,000. Exact per-customer calculation is shown in the attached spreadsheet tab "CreditsExample".

- Latency measurement boundary: Percentiles are computed on full request lifecycle measured at the ingress-to-egress path within our edge region (start-of-request timestamp to final response write). Network latency beyond our egress (client network) is not included.

- Monthly machine-readable report: We can provide a CSV export with minute-level availability, per-region breakdowns, p95/p99 series, and incident annotations. We commit to delivering the export within 5 business days of month close for hosted customers. For Dedicated customers we provide extended logs under separate agreement.

Operational notes and proposed contract language (summary):

1) Measurement: "Availability" is computed at the API ingress as percent of successful (2xx) requests after up to 3 automatic retries within 1s total retry window. Outage minute is counted if availability < 99% for that minute. Monthly availability computed per calendar month.

2) Credits: Use the banded credit table provided earlier. Credits calculated on invoice line items for Redwood-hosted inference usage only. Maximum credit per month is 50% of that invoice line.

3) Scheduled maintenance: Excluded when >72 hours notice and published to Console. Emergency maintenance excluded only for events that meet our documented emergency criteria; these are rare and will be disclosed in incident reports.

4) Reporting: We will deliver CSV exports and a summary PDF with the monthly SLA metrics within 5 business days. We can automate delivery to a shared GCS/Drive folder or via secure SFTP per your preference.

I'll attach the updated spreadsheet with the retry-policy values and credit examples. Rafael and Monica are available to run your SRE team through the worksheets on Tuesday afternoon if that works. If FinPlus is comfortable, we can convert the measurement methodology into Exhibit A for signature and then have legal iterate on the credit caps.

Thanks — talk soon.

Karthik Iyer
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
karthik.iyer@redwood.com
+1 (650) 555-0199

--
Karthik Iyer
Redwood Inference

Subject: RFP: SLA & Measurement Methodology Clarifications (Section 3)

Hi Karthik,

Thanks for submitting Redwood's RFP responses. Our procurement and SRE teams reviewed Section 3 (SLA/SLO). We have a few clarifications before we advance to legal:

1) Uptime commitment: You listed 99.95% monthly availability for the hosted inference API. Please confirm whether that is measured at the HTTP 200 success rate at the edge, or at the backend GPU serving layer.
2) Latency SLOs: For the 95th/99th percentile latency targets you provided, what's the sampling window and traffic weighting? Do you exclude warmups or preflight checks?
3) Measurement methodology: Provide the formula/aggregation method you will use to calculate downtime and p95/p99 (e.g., rolling window, calendar month, what constitutes an outage incident).
4) Service credits: How are credits calculated? Are credits capped per month and per incident?
5) Scheduled maintenance: Define notification windows and whether maintenance is excluded from uptime calculation.

Could you respond with our requested detail as a single document (or spreadsheet) we can attach to the contract? Our target is to resolve these items by EOD Monday so legal can redline the Master Services Agreement on Tuesday.

Laura Chen

laura.chen@finplus.com
+1 (415) 555-1298


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Laura Chen <laura.chen@finplus.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 14 Feb 2026 13:45:00 -0800
Subject: Re: RFP: SLA & Measurement Methodology Clarifications (Section 3)

Laura —

Thanks for the clear list. We've pulled together a consolidated response and an attached workbook that details our measurement formulas and sample calculations. High level answers below; detailed definitions are in the attached PDF and the spreadsheet (see attachments).

1) Uptime measurement: We measure availability at the API gateway edge (HTTP-level) after retries. This reflects customer-observed successful completions. Backend serving-layer losses (e.g., a single GPU host failure masked by retry/fallback) are visible in our telemetry but availability is computed at the edge. See attachment Redwood-SLA-Response-20260214.pdf, Section 1.

2) Latency SLOs: Percentiles are computed on successful request completion times measured at the ingress gateway. We calculate p95 and p99 over a rolling 30-day calendar window aggregated per calendar month for reporting. Requests that fail (non-2xx) are excluded from p95/p99 latency calculations but are counted toward availability. We exclude synthetic warmup traffic from production sampling via request tags; those requests are filtered out before percentile aggregation.

3) Measurement methodology: Downtime is defined as any minute in which the edge availability drops below 99% after automatic retries defined in our client retry policy. Monthly availability = (Total minutes in calendar month - outage minutes) / Total minutes in month. P95/P99 use the joined sample of all successful user requests, weighted equally (no per-customer weighting). See spreadsheet tab "Formulas" for exact SQL-like pseudo-code and example calculations.

4) Service credits: Credits are calculated as a percentage of monthly invoice for Redwood-hosted inference, per the following bands: 99.95-100% = 0% credit; 99.9-99.949% = 10% credit; 99.5-99.899% = 25% credit; <99.5% = 50% credit. Credits are capped at 50% per calendar month and apply only to Redwood-hosted plan usage charges. For multi-tenant incidents affecting multiple customers, credits are prorated by affected usage minutes. Single-customer incidents where the customer has Dedicated capacity are handled under Dedicated terms (not this credit table).

5) Scheduled maintenance: Planned maintenance windows of up to 4 hours per quarter are excluded from uptime calculations when we provide >72 hours advance notice via the Console and the scheduled maintenance is published to the customer's SLA notifications. Emergency maintenance is not excluded.

Next steps: Attached are (1) Redwood-SLA-Response-20260214.pdf with definitions and sample SQL pseudo-code, and (2) SLA-Measurement-Methodology.xlsx with worked examples. We're happy to take this into contract as an exhibit. If you want, Rafael and I can run a 30-minute SRE walkthrough with your team Monday morning to walk through the sample calculations.

Best,

Karthik Iyer
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
karthik.iyer@redwood.com
+1 (650) 555-0199

Attachments: Redwood-SLA-Response-20260214.pdf (application/pdf), SLA-Measurement-Methodology.xlsx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet)

title: Committed spend — annual prepay invoicing & drawdown tracking
preview:
From: Sasha Ivanov <sasha@datawave.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Datawave Billing <billing@datawave.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: Annual prepay invoicing & VAT handling for committed spend

Hi Marissa,

Thanks again for the productive call yesterday. We are ready to move forward with the committed-spend option you outlined (12-month prepay, committed USD 200,000). Before we sign, our finance team needs an invoicing schedule and confirmation of how Redwood will issue invoices for the annual prepayment (single upfront invoice vs. staggered invoices), plus how VAT will be handled for our EU entities.

Specifically:
- Can you provide the proposed invoice dates and what the invoice reference will be for our AP (so we can attach to the PO)?
- If we prepay the full amount, will you provide a drawdown statement we can reconcile monthly?
- For VAT: Datawave EMEA will handle VAT reverse-charge if Redwood confirms the correct VAT ID and supplies a compliant invoice.

We typically need an invoice stub and a standard remittance instruction to submit to our bank for wire.

Would appreciate these docs so we can route a PO internally.

Thanks,
Sasha
Sasha Ivanov
Head of Finance Ops | Datawave Inc.
[signed]

--
[Attachment: Datawave_PO_Template_67890.pdf (PDF, 42KB)]

From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
To: Sasha Ivanov <sasha@datawave.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 10:32:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Annual prepay invoicing & VAT handling for committed spend

Hi Sasha — great to hear you're moving forward. Attached is the invoice template we typically use for annual prepays and a sample drawdown tracker (Excel). High-level proposal below: 

- Invoice timing: single upfront invoice issued on contract signature date (net 30 by default) for the full committed amount (USD 200,000).
- Drawdown: we maintain a monthly drawdown ledger showing consumed credits and remaining balance. We’ll share a live sheet and a PDF statement at month-end.
- Reconciliation: quarterly usage reconciliation with any top-ups or credits applied.
- VAT: Redwood will issue the invoice to the entity you indicate. If Datawave EMEA is listed and provides a valid VAT ID, we can mark the invoice appropriately for reverse-charge. Please confirm the Billing Entity and VAT ID.

Next steps I suggest:
1) Rafael (cc) will share wire instructions and the formal invoice PDF after signature.
2) We’ll send a DocuSign packet for the SOW + payment terms.
3) Provide your PO number and billing entity details so the invoice references correctly.

I attached: RDW_InvoiceTemplate_AnnualPrepay.xlsx and a one-page drawdown sample.

Best,
Marissa Cole
Head of Strategic Accounts | Redwood Inference
Direct: +1 (415) 555-0199

--
[Attachment: RDW_InvoiceTemplate_AnnualPrepay.xlsx (Excel spreadsheet, 18KB)]

From: Rafael Mendes <rafael_mendes@redwood.com>
To: Sasha Ivanov <sasha@datawave.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan_blake@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Feb 2026 09:04:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Annual prepay invoicing & VAT handling for committed spend

Hi Sasha — Rafael from Finance here. Thanks Marissa. A few practical items from our side so AP can process quickly: 

1) Invoice reference
- We will issue: INV-RDW-RDW-Datawave-RDW-202602
- Please include PO# on the remittance (Sasha, you can reply with it and we’ll update).

2) Payment and bank details
- Wire to: Redwood Inference, Bank of Pacific — Acct: ********1234, Routing: 121000358
- Swift: BOPBUS33
- Remittance email: billing@redwood.com
(Full wire instructions attached as a text file for AP upload.)

3) DocuSign and timeline
- We’ll send DocuSign for the SOW + invoice acceptance today. Once signed, we issue the invoice the same business day.

4) Drawdown tracking
- We maintain a monthly drawdown ledger on a shared Google Sheet. One row per month with starting balance, tokens consumed (or dollars invoiced by usage), and closing balance. We will grant edit access to your billing contact and produce a PDF statement each month-end.

5) VAT / tax
- For EU reverse charge: include your VAT ID and billing country on the purchase order. We’ll include the VAT note and your VAT ID on the invoice. If Datawave provides a UK VAT ID (if applicable), the invoice will reference reverse-charge language.

Attachments: Wire_Instructions_RDW.txt, Signed_SOW_Datawave_RDW.pdf (SOW pending signature), and the drawdown sample.

Let me know if your AP needs any additional fields on the invoice (e.g., cost center, project code).

Thanks,
Rafael Mendes
Finance Manager | Redwood Inference

--
[Attachment: Wire_Instructions_RDW.txt (text, 1.2KB)]

Date: Wed, 11 Feb 2026 13:15:00 -08:00
Subject: Re: Annual prepay invoicing & VAT handling for committed spend

Sasha / Rafael / Marissa — quick legal note on VAT and PO phrasing: 

- If the billing entity is Datawave EMEA and they provide a valid VAT ID, Redwood will apply the reverse-charge note on the invoice. Please confirm whether Datawave is acting as the importer of record.
- If Datawave requires us to charge VAT (for any reason), please flag immediately and include a VAT-registered billing address in the PO.
- For US-based entities: provide the company legal name and W-9 if required by your AP rules.

We prefer the PO to reference the SOW number (SOW-RDW-2026-01) and the HubSpot deal id (RDW-5493) for easy cross-reference. This reduces delays during payment posting.

Thanks,
Naomi Feldman
Deputy General Counsel | Redwood Inference

title: SLA redlines: uptime & support for Acme Health MSA
preview:
From: Taylor Brooks <taylor.brooks@acmehealth.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nidhi Patel <n.patel@acmehealth.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 09:12:00 -0800
Subject: ACME - Order Form & SLA redlines

Marissa,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Attached is our annotated Order Form and a short set of redlines to the SLA in the MSA. Two items we'd like to resolve this week so procurement can issue a PO:

- Uptime: we proposed 99.95% for critical production endpoints (Table A). Please confirm whether Redwood can commit or provide an equivalent higher-tier offering.
- Service credits: the matrix in section 3.2 was adjusted to make credits on a sliding scale starting at 0.5% for <99.95, 2% for <99.9, and 5% for <99.0. We asked for a cap tied to monthly fees.

Also, our security team flagged maintenance windows that could occur during our business hours. We prefer maintenance to be scheduled outside 7am-7pm ET and receive at least 72 hours notice for planned maintenance.

Attached: ACME_OrderForm_Redlines_v3.docx and ACME_Redlines_annotated.pdf.

Can you route this to your legal and ops folks and let us know Redwood's position by EOD Wednesday?

Thanks,

Taylor Brooks
Senior Procurement Manager
Acme Health
taylor.brooks@acmehealth.com
(415) 555-0192

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwoodinference.com>
To: Taylor Brooks <taylor.brooks@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 11:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ACME - Order Form & SLA redlines

Taylor —

Appreciate the fast turnaround and clear asks. High level positions from Redwood below; I've attached our suggested tracked-changes for the SLA (Redwood_Proposed_SLA_v1.docx).

1) Uptime: Our standard Hosted API SLA is 99.9% for production endpoints. We can offer 99.95% as a paid add-on for Dedicated reserved pools (requires capacity commitment). If Acme is open to Dedicated or a committed monthly spend, we can surface a 99.95% uptime guarantee in the Order Form.

2) Service credits: We're comfortable with a sliding credit matrix but propose the following: 5% credit for monthly availability <99.0, 2% for <99.5, 1% for <99.9, and no credit for >99.9. We also prefer a cap of 20% of monthly fees. See attached redline.

3) Response times: For Severity 1 incidents we propose 1-hour acknowledgement and 4-hour remediation window with a 24/7 on-call rotation for Dedicated customers. For Hosted customers, standard response is 2-hour acknowledgement and next-business-hour remediation. Please let us know which category Acme expects.

4) Maintenance windows: We can commit to scheduling routine maintenance outside of 8am–6pm PT (which maps to 11am–9pm ET). For critical security patches we reserve the right to push urgent maintenance with 8 hours' notice; planned maintenance will have 72 hours' notice.

If Acme needs the tighter 99.95% uptime for Hosted traffic, we can propose a hybrid: prefix routing to Dedicated pool for critical endpoints (costed) while keeping non-critical queries on Hosted.

Priyom and Rafael are looped in for legal/ops specifics; happy to hop on a 30-minute call to align. If you can share which product endpoints need the 99.95% SLA, that will help us scope capacity and pricing.

Best,

Marissa Cole
Enterprise Account Executive
Redwood Inference
marissa@redwoodinference.com
+1 (650) 555-0133

From: Taylor Brooks <taylor.brooks@acmehealth.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Nidhi Patel <n.patel@acmehealth.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Feb 2026 10:30:00 -0800
Subject: Re: ACME - Order Form & SLA redlines

Marissa,

Thanks — that's helpful. A few quick clarifications from our side after reviewing your proposal with engineering and legal:

- Uptime: Acme needs 99.95% for our patient-facing inference endpoints. We're willing to sign up for a committed monthly block for a subset of traffic; can you provide an estimated pricing delta (ballpark) for moving 20% of our tokens to a Dedicated pool? If that's feasible, we can accept the 99.95% add-on.

- Service credits: Your proposed banding looks fine but legal wants a clearer calculation example and wants the monthly cap spelled out in the Order Form. Please add an example row showing calculation for a $50k monthly invoice.

- Response times: We need Severity 1 remediation within 4 hours and 1-hour acknowledgement. That aligns with your Dedicated offering — can you confirm that this will be in the Order Form and in the SOW?

title: Rerank & embeddings evaluation — next steps (Crestline Analytics)
preview:
From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Emma Lee <emma@crestline.ai>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 15:12:00 -0800
Subject: Rerank & embeddings evaluation — next steps (Crestline Analytics)

Hi Emma — great call earlier. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we stay aligned:

Recap:
- Crestline uses a hybrid retrieval stack (Elasticsearch + ANN) for product search; current reranker is TensorFlow-based on top-20 candidates.
- Key success metrics you care about: recall@10, recall@1 for conversions, retrieval latency (95th percentile), and per-query cost.
- Typical request distribution: ~70% short queries (2-4 tokens), 30% long queries (6-20 tokens).

What we propose for the competitive evaluation:
1) A/B rerank test: Redwood inference (quantized 8-bit embeddings + fine-tuned reranker) vs HyperscalerX text-embedding-ada-like baseline vs your current on-prem stack.
2) Dataset: 50k documents, 5k held-out queries (you mentioned you can provide a sample subset).
3) Metrics: recall@1, recall@5, recall@10, MRR, retrieval latency (p50/p95), token cost and per-query cost normalized to 1M queries.
4) Embedding dim variants to test: 1024 and 1536 (we'll include both to see tradeoffs).
5) Similarity functions: cosine vs dot-product.

Technical artifacts attached: test-config.yaml (defines batching, quantization profile, candidate pool sizes) — see attachment.

Requests for you:
- Please share a small sample S3 link with 50k docs (or a representative 5k if you want to start smaller) and the 5k query set.
- Confirm any PII requirements/redaction we should apply before ingestion.

Proposed timeline:
- Prep + ingest: 3 business days after we receive sample data
- Run: 2 business days per round (we plan 2 rounds: initial + tuned)
- Results + deep-dive: within 2 business days after run

If that looks good I can get the infra and a 45-min deep-dive on the calendar with Rafael and Vivek to align on configuration and success criteria.

Thanks — excited to dig in.

—
Marissa Cole
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0124

Attachment: test-config.yaml (defines batch_size, candidate_pool=20/100/1000, quantization=8bit, embeddings=[1024,1536])

From: Emma Lee <emma@crestline.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Feb 2026 16:05:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Rerank & embeddings evaluation — next steps (Crestline Analytics)

Thanks Marissa — that recap is perfect and matches what we discussed. A few clarifications:

- We can share an S3 prefix with 60k product documents (jsonl) and a 5k query set. I'll send credentials via SecureShare (I can't paste creds in email).
- PII: product descriptions occasionally contain vendor contact emails; we require that vendor emails be redacted during ingestion. That is non-negotiable for our security team.
- On hypotheses: we expect higher-dim embeddings to improve recall@10 but worried about latency/cost tradeoffs; happy to see both numbers.
- Can Redwood provide an estimated cost delta for 1536 vs 1024 dims (tokens / inference cost per 1M queries)?

I'd like to schedule the deep-dive next Wednesday or Thursday afternoon PT. Also looping in my platform engineer (CloudOps team) for any infra/credentials coordination — fyi.

Best,
Emma
Emma Lee
Head of Search & Recommendations, Crestline Analytics
emma@crestline.ai

Date: Wed, 11 Feb 2026 09:22:00 -0800
Subject: Re: Rerank & embeddings evaluation — next steps (Crestline Analytics)

Marissa / Vivek — small suggestions from the solutions side before we lock config:

- Add recall@1 and recall@5 as explicitly required metrics (these are decisive for conversion).
- Candidate pool sizes: run with top-20 (to match current) and top-100 to simulate broader retrieval.
- Embedding dims: test 1024 and 1536; quantify index size per million vectors and estimate memory footprint (we should record approx GB per 1M).
- Similarity: run cosine and dot-product — some ANNs are faster with dot-product depending on quantization.
- Latency: capture both P50 and P95 for retrieval and rerank separately (we've seen rerank stage dominate).
- Cost tracking: report CPU/GPU-hours and estimate $/1M queries for each configuration; include storage cost for indices.

I attached a CSV template (crestline_eval_results_template.csv) to capture all metrics consistently. I'll own the run orchestration with Vivek if Emma provides the S3 link and credentials.

title: Asteria Therapeutics — DPA questions (subprocessors, KMS, EU transfers)
preview:
From: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@asteria-tx.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
Cc: Marco Bernal <marco.bernal@asteria-tx.com>, procurement@asteria-tx.com
Date: Tue, 3 Feb 2026 09:14:22 -0500
Subject: Questions on DPA: subprocessors, KMS, and no-training wording

Hi Rafael,

Thanks for sending the draft DPA earlier. Our security and privacy teams reviewed it and had a few clarifying questions before we can proceed to signature:

1) Subprocessor list & notification cadence — can you confirm how you notify customers of new subprocessors and whether customers can require prior approval for specific classes (e.g., analytics vs. hosting)?

2) KMS / Key residency — we need to understand options for onshore encryption keys for EU data (customer-managed KMS or HSM). Is that supported in Redwood Private, and are there additional costs or operational steps?

3) No-training / model improvement — the DPA has an opt-out but our legal team wants explicit language ensuring prompts/responses will not be used to improve models or derivatives produced for us. Can you confirm the operational controls you use to segregate or exclude opt-out accounts from any training pipelines?

4) Cross-border transfers — we are EU-based and need to confirm reliance on SCCs (and an attestation that no transfers happen to countries without adequacy, except under SCCs).

Would appreciate a short call this week to walk through these items. I’ve looped in Marco (security) and procurement to coordinate.

Best,
Elena
Elena Rossi
Head of Security & Privacy
Asteria Therapeutics

Attachments: Asteria_DPA_review_notes.pdf (annotated)


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@asteria-tx.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Marco Bernal <marco.bernal@asteria-tx.com>
Date: Tue, 3 Feb 2026 12:08:05 -05:00
Subject: Re: Questions on DPA: subprocessors, KMS, and no-training wording

Hi Elena — thanks for the clear notes. I’ll address each point below and propose a short follow-up call (30 min) Thurs 10:00 CET / 04:00 ET — let me know if that works for your team.

1) Subprocessor notifications: Redwood maintains a published subprocessor registry (linked in our DPA). We notify customers by email 30 days prior to onboarding any new subprocessor used for production inference. For high-sensitivity classes (we classify as: hosting, telemetry/analytics, logging), we can offer an approval workflow for enterprise contracts (this is enabled by Default for Dedicated/Private customers). I’ve attached the current registry snapshot and our notification process (Redwood_DPA-exhibitB_kms_flow.pdf).

2) KMS / Key residency: For Redwood Private we support customer-managed KMS (bring-your-own-key) and on-prem HSM options. For Hosted and Dedicated we offer KMS integrations with region-restricted keys (customer-managed in supported cloud regions) and an onshore routing option for EU-only keys. There is an operational onboarding checklist and one-time integration fee for HSM setup in Private. Technical lead Jonas can add details on envelope encryption and key lifecycle.

3) No-training / model improvement operational controls: short answer — yes, we operationalize no-training by: an ingestion-level tag that marks requests as "no-learn", an isolated routing path that excludes opt-out traffic from training/telemetry ingestion, and a retention policy (ephemeral KV cache for opt-out requests unless otherwise requested). For proof, we produce an evidence pack (SCCs-declaration.pdf + infra diagrams) and can commit to a contractual clause that explicitly bars the use of prompts/responses for model improvement and derivative datasets. Kimberly — would you like us to propose a redline for stronger explicit phrasing?

4) Cross-border transfers: we rely on the EU SCCs for transfers outside the EU and can provide an SCC Annex with mapping of subprocessors and locations. For Private deployments, data need not leave the customer VPC unless you opt-in for Redwood-managed backups (which then follow SCCs).

Call options: Thu 2026-02-05 10:00 CET / 04:00 ET or Fri 2026-02-06 14:00 CET / 10:00 ET.

Thanks — happy to walk through the evidence pack.

Cheers,
Rafael Mendes
Head of Sales, Enterprise
Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: Redwood_DPA-exhibitB_kms_flow.pdf, SCCs-declaration.pdf


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@asteria-tx.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Marco Bernal <marco.bernal@asteria-tx.com>
Date: Wed, 4 Feb 2026 09:46:50 -05:00
Subject: Re: Questions on DPA: subprocessors, KMS, and no-training wording

Hi Elena / Marco — I’m Kimberly from Legal & Privacy. Rafael summarized things well; below are concrete redline suggestions we can commit to in the DPA (I’ll attach a tracked-change doc to the Drive link):

A) No-training clause (recommended wording): "Redwood agrees that Customer Data, including prompts and model responses associated with Customer accounts for which Customer has asserted a 'no-training' designation, will not be used to train, fine-tune, improve, or otherwise update any machine learning models, nor will such data be included in any datasets used for model development or research, except as expressly permitted in a separate written agreement."

B) Evidence & auditability: Redwood will provide an operational evidence pack upon request showing: subprocessor list and locations, routing policies for no-training traffic, logs (redacted) of no-training tags for a two-week sample, and KMS integration checklist.

C) Subprocessor approval: For the Asteria enterprise tier, insert an appendix allowing Asteria to object to a new subprocessor within 15 days of notice; if objection is raised, Redwood will (i) provide mitigation steps, or (ii) pause onboarding until resolved.

D) Transfers: Include SCCs Annex and confirm Redwood will not transfer EU personal data to jurisdictions without an adequacy decision except under SCCs or customer direction.

Operational note to Jonas: please confirm the retention windows for opt-out request logs and whether we can produce a two-week sample within 3 business days for audit.

I’ll upload a marked-up DPA (redlines_Asteria_DPA_v1.docx) to the Drive folder and send a DocuSign envelope once Rafael confirms the final text with Elena.

Regards,
Kimberly Park
Director, Legal & Privacy
Redwood Inference

Redwood Inference | kimberly.park@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0148

text:
From: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@asteria-tx.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
Cc: Marco Bernal <marco.bernal@asteria-tx.com>, procurement@asteria-tx.com
Date: Tue, 3 Feb 2026 09:14:22 -0500
Subject: Questions on DPA: subprocessors, KMS, and no-training wording

Hi Rafael,

Thanks for sending the draft DPA earlier. Our security and privacy teams reviewed it and had a few clarifying questions before we can proceed to signature:

1) Subprocessor list & notification cadence — can you confirm how you notify customers of new subprocessors and whether customers can require prior approval for specific classes (e.g., analytics vs. hosting)?

2) KMS / Key residency — we need to understand options for onshore encryption keys for EU data (customer-managed KMS or HSM). Is that supported in Redwood Private, and are there additional costs or operational steps?

3) No-training / model improvement — the DPA has an opt-out but our legal team wants explicit language ensuring prompts/responses will not be used to improve models or derivatives produced for us. Can you confirm the operational controls you use to segregate or exclude opt-out accounts from any training pipelines?

4) Cross-border transfers — we are EU-based and need to confirm reliance on SCCs (and an attestation that no transfers happen to countries without adequacy, except under SCCs).

Would appreciate a short call this week to walk through these items. I’ve looped in Marco (security) and procurement to coordinate.

Best,
Elena
Elena Rossi
Head of Security & Privacy
Asteria Therapeutics

Attachments: Asteria_DPA_review_notes.pdf (annotated)


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@asteria-tx.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Marco Bernal <marco.bernal@asteria-tx.com>
Date: Tue, 3 Feb 2026 12:08:05 -05:00
Subject: Re: Questions on DPA: subprocessors, KMS, and no-training wording

Hi Elena — thanks for the clear notes. I’ll address each point below and propose a short follow-up call (30 min) Thurs 10:00 CET / 04:00 ET — let me know if that works for your team.

1) Subprocessor notifications: Redwood maintains a published subprocessor registry (linked in our DPA). We notify customers by email 30 days prior to onboarding any new subprocessor used for production inference. For high-sensitivity classes (we classify as: hosting, telemetry/analytics, logging), we can offer an approval workflow for enterprise contracts (this is enabled by Default for Dedicated/Private customers). I’ve attached the current registry snapshot and our notification process (Redwood_DPA-exhibitB_kms_flow.pdf).

2) KMS / Key residency: For Redwood Private we support customer-managed KMS (bring-your-own-key) and on-prem HSM options. For Hosted and Dedicated we offer KMS integrations with region-restricted keys (customer-managed in supported cloud regions) and an onshore routing option for EU-only keys. There is an operational onboarding checklist and one-time integration fee for HSM setup in Private. Technical lead Jonas can add details on envelope encryption and key lifecycle.

3) No-training / model improvement operational controls: short answer — yes, we operationalize no-training by: an ingestion-level tag that marks requests as "no-learn", an isolated routing path that excludes opt-out traffic from training/telemetry ingestion, and a retention policy (ephemeral KV cache for opt-out requests unless otherwise requested). For proof, we produce an evidence pack (SCCs-declaration.pdf + infra diagrams) and can commit to a contractual clause that explicitly bars the use of prompts/responses for model improvement and derivative datasets. Kimberly — would you like us to propose a redline for stronger explicit phrasing?

4) Cross-border transfers: we rely on the EU SCCs for transfers outside the EU and can provide an SCC Annex with mapping of subprocessors and locations. For Private deployments, data need not leave the customer VPC unless you opt-in for Redwood-managed backups (which then follow SCCs).

Call options: Thu 2026-02-05 10:00 CET / 04:00 ET or Fri 2026-02-06 14:00 CET / 10:00 ET.

Thanks — happy to walk through the evidence pack.

Cheers,
Rafael Mendes
Head of Sales, Enterprise
Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: Redwood_DPA-exhibitB_kms_flow.pdf, SCCs-declaration.pdf


From: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@asteria-tx.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwood.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Marco Bernal <marco.bernal@asteria-tx.com>
Date: Wed, 4 Feb 2026 09:46:50 -05:00
Subject: Re: Questions on DPA: subprocessors, KMS, and no-training wording

Hi Elena / Marco — I’m Kimberly from Legal & Privacy. Rafael summarized things well; below are concrete redline suggestions we can commit to in the DPA (I’ll attach a tracked-change doc to the Drive link):

A) No-training clause (recommended wording): "Redwood agrees that Customer Data, including prompts and model responses associated with Customer accounts for which Customer has asserted a 'no-training' designation, will not be used to train, fine-tune, improve, or otherwise update any machine learning models, nor will such data be included in any datasets used for model development or research, except as expressly permitted in a separate written agreement."

B) Evidence & auditability: Redwood will provide an operational evidence pack upon request showing: subprocessor list and locations, routing policies for no-training traffic, logs (redacted) of no-training tags for a two-week sample, and KMS integration checklist.

C) Subprocessor approval: For the Asteria enterprise tier, insert an appendix allowing Asteria to object to a new subprocessor within 15 days of notice; if objection is raised, Redwood will (i) provide mitigation steps, or (ii) pause onboarding until resolved.

D) Transfers: Include SCCs Annex and confirm Redwood will not transfer EU personal data to jurisdictions without an adequacy decision except under SCCs or customer direction.

Operational note to Jonas: please confirm the retention windows for opt-out request logs and whether we can produce a two-week sample within 3 business days for audit.

I’ll upload a marked-up DPA (redlines_Asteria_DPA_v1.docx) to the Drive folder and send a DocuSign envelope once Rafael confirms the final text with Elena.

Regards,
Kimberly Park
Director, Legal & Privacy
Redwood Inference

What I need today:
1) Neha/Sean: confirm we’re comfortable with the measurement language + exclusions (esp. streaming disconnect classification + fallbacks).
2) CS/SE: feedback on customer-facing framing and the “what will you see in reporting” section.

Key changes since v5:
- Explicitly separates internal SLO targets (engineering) vs contractual SLA (credits + definitions).

- Clarifies that routing fallback is a mitigation mechanism; SLI accounting is based on what the customer experiences (i.e., final response status + end-to-end latency).

Proposed customer comms plan (Dedicated only for now):
- Email to Dedicated admins + posted in Console (banner + link to docs) the week we flip dashboards on.
- Includes escalation expectations: what data to provide, and response times during incidents.

If we can land this by EOW, I’ll route through Legal for final review and prep the outbound list w/ CS.

Lauren

- The “what doesn’t count” bullets are clear.

Edits/requests:
1) Add a very explicit line: "This does not change your current contract unless you have an executed amendment." We’ve had customers assume anything emailed is contractual.

Date: Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 10:28 AM PST
Subject: Re: Dedicated SLA mapping + customer comms plan (tiered SLO rollout)

+1 to Rachel’s notes.

Two SE-specific callouts (based on recent “streaming disconnects” questions):
- We need a short FAQ snippet defining “client abort” vs “server disconnect.” Customers see a broken stream and assume it always counts against us. If we’re going to exclude client aborts, we should provide how they can confirm (e.g., SDK error codes / TCP reset vs cancel).
- The mapping doc uses “successful request” language. For streaming, some customers will ask: is a partial stream “success”? Suggest we define success as: stream completes normally and we emit a final completion event (or equivalent). Anything else is counted as failure unless it’s a client cancel.

Also: I recommend we do a 15-min enablement with Solutions Eng + CS the day before the email goes out. Otherwise field teams will get pulled into long debates.

Markus
Markus Klein
Director, Solutions Engineering

Date: Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 11:11 AM PST
Subject: Re: Dedicated SLA mapping + customer comms plan (tiered SLO rollout)

On measurement/exclusions:

- The “doesn’t count” list in v6 is broadly aligned with the SLI spec we’re implementing.
- One nuance: retries/fallback. We should avoid saying "fallback does not count as an error." What matters is the customer-visible outcome. If we fail over and return a valid response, it’s a success for availability SLI; latency SLI is still end-to-end. If we fail over but still 5xx, it’s a failure.
- For streaming: we can commit to counting “server-side stream termination” as a failure event. Client cancels are excluded (we’ll key off cancel event + connection close pattern).

I can add a short “Streaming SLI semantics” paragraph for Elliot to fold into docs.

Aisha
Aisha Bello
Senior SRE (SLO/Error Budgets)

Agree with Sean.

- “In rare fleet-wide incidents, we may take actions (traffic shaping, failover) to protect overall service while meeting tier commitments.”

- The “doesn’t count” list in v6 is broadly aligned with the SLI spec we’re implementing.
- One nuance: retries/fallback. We should avoid saying "fallback does not count as an error." What matters is the customer-visible outcome. If we fail over and return a valid response, it’s a success for availability SLI; latency SLI is still end-to-end. If we fail over but still 5xx, it’s a failure.
- For streaming: we can commit to counting “server-side stream termination” as a failure event. Client cancels are excluded (we’ll key off cancel event + connection close pattern).

I can add a short “Streaming SLI semantics” paragraph for Elliot to fold into docs.

Aisha
Aisha Bello
Senior SRE (SLO/Error Budgets)


---

From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwoodinference.com>, Aisha Bello <aisha.bello@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Markus Klein <markus.klein@redwoodinference.com>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwoodinference.com>, Elliot Price <elliot.price@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 12:03 PM PST
Subject: Re: Dedicated SLA mapping + customer comms plan (tiered SLO rollout)

Approved in principle.

Two requested edits before this goes to Legal / external:
1) In the SLA mapping doc, replace “we guarantee p99 latency” type language (even if it’s in a table header). Use “SLO reporting includes p50/p95/p99 latency; SLA is availability-based (unless contract says otherwise).” We’ve been burned by customers screenshotting internal targets.
2) Add a one-liner on error budget status: “Used internally to guide mitigation and release safety; not a contractual threshold.” (At least for this phase.)

Otherwise, this is the right direction and will reduce ambiguity in escalations.

Sean
Sean Gallagher
Director, Site Reliability Engineering

title: Re: Incident bridge template + consistent Jira SUP linking (INT-332 / INT-333)
preview:
From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 9:12 AM
Subject: Incident bridge template + consistent Jira SUP linking (INT-332 / INT-333)

Rafa/Sean/Jasmine,

Quick coordination ask for the known-issues sprint + playbook refresh workstream.

We’re updating the Support bridge workflow so that when Support spins up a bridge for an escalation, it’s consistent and discoverable after the fact. This is tied to:
- INT-332 (Slack automation for support bridges)
- INT-333 (Jira automation: SLA reminders + required fields + prompts to link KB/known-issues)

Proposed: we standardize a “Bridge kickoff” pinned message template *and* a rule for how we link SUP tickets.

Questions / what I need from SRE:
1) Required fields for a Support-created bridge kickoff message
   - customer name + tier (Hosted/Dedicated/Private)
   - region
   - model + version (if pinned)
   - SDK + client env (if relevant)
   - timestamps + request IDs (min 3)
   - symptoms + current impact
   - Support priority (P0–P4)
   - current hypothesis + what we’ve tried
   - links: SUP ticket, dashboards (Support-safe), status page (if applicable)

2) Jira linking convention
   - Do we require “SUP created before bridge” always?
   - If multiple SUPs: do we pick a primary and link others? Or consolidate?
   - Where should the Slack channel link live (custom field vs comment)?

3) Channel naming + topic format
   - Suggestion: #sup-bridge-<yyyymmdd>-<cust>-<short-issue>
   - Topic: “P# | SUP-#### | <cust> | <region> | owner: <name> | next update: <time>”

If you can reply with “yes/no + edits” today, we can finalize the template and hand it to Benji for the workflow + to the Jira automation owner.

Thanks,
Dev

—
Dev Patel
Support Ops Lead
Redwood Inference


On Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 11:03 AM Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 11:03 AM
Subject: Re: Incident bridge template + consistent Jira SUP linking (INT-332 / INT-333)

+1 to standardizing this.

Answers / proposals from on-call side:

(1) Required bridge kickoff fields (SRE minimum)
Keep it short, but the “can’t work without it” set is:
- SUP key (must exist) + link
- customer + tier
- region
- endpoint (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank) + streaming yes/no
- model + requested max_tokens (if latency/timeout)
- time window (start time in UTC)
- request IDs (>=3) + any correlation IDs if available
- current customer-visible impact (what broke / what degraded)
- what changed recently (SDK upgrade? deploy? traffic spike?) if known

Optional but helpful:
- “What does success look like in 30–60 min?” (mitigation goal)
- dashboard links (Support-safe quick view)

(2) Jira linking convention
- Yes: “SUP created before bridge” should be the rule. If Support needs to pull in on-call, we need a trackable artifact immediately.
- If multiple SUPs for same event: pick one primary SUP (the one with the best repro / highest tier), and link the rest as “is related to” in Jira. Don’t try to merge tickets mid-incident.
- Slack channel link should live in a dedicated field if we have it; if not, add it in the SUP description at top under a “Bridge/Links” section + also drop a comment when you first add on-call.

(3) Channel naming + topic
Naming looks fine. Topic proposal:
“SUP-#### | P# | <cust> | <tier> | <region> | <primary symptom> | owner: <Support name> | oncall: <name> | next update: <time>”

Also request for INT-332:
- The workflow should auto-post the kickoff template and pin it.
- It should prompt for the SUP key and then set it in the channel topic (or at least include it in the pinned kickoff).

Last: when this turns into a real incident (multi-customer, clear service degradation), Support should move it to #incidents per the playbook rather than running parallel bridges. But the linking pattern should be the same either way.

Rafael

—
Rafael Mendes
Site Reliability Engineering
Redwood Inference


On Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 12:26 PM Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 12:26 PM
Subject: Re: Incident bridge template + consistent Jira SUP linking (INT-332 / INT-333)

Agree with Rafa’s list. Two adds that reduce back-and-forth with on-call:

- “Recent deploys / runtime release in last 24h?” (yes/no/unknown). Even “unknown” helps.
- “Any routing fallback active?” (Support can usually answer via the quick metrics view; if we don’t have that yet, at least add a checkbox line in the template). This ties to the ongoing work to make fallback signals Support-visible.

Re: SUP before bridge — yes. The bridge kickoff message should include the SUP link on the first line so it’s obvious.

Re: #incidents vs #support — please add one line to the kickoff template:
“If this looks multi-customer or regional, escalate to #incidents + page SRE per Sev policy.”

We should avoid a pattern where Support pages SRE without enough context; the template solves that.

Sean

—
Sean Gallagher
SRE Manager
Redwood Inference


On Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 1:41 PM Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 1:41 PM
Subject: Re: Incident bridge template + consistent Jira SUP linking (INT-332 / INT-333)

This is super clear—thanks.

Action items I’m taking:
- Update the kickoff template to reflect the “minimum viable” fields (SUP link first line, endpoint/streaming, UTC window, request IDs, impact, recent change, fallback signal)
- Add the line about moving to #incidents when it’s multi-customer/regional
- Add a “primary SUP + related SUPs” note and how to link in Jira

One follow-up on INT-333 (SLA automation):
If a ticket is marked “Waiting on Customer,” do we want automation to stop nagging for updates, or still require internal updates at the normal cadence for P0/P1?

I’m leaning:
- pause external update SLA when Waiting on Customer
- keep internal checkpoint reminders (e.g., every hour for P0) so we don’t drop it

Let me know if that conflicts with on-call expectations.

Dev


On Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 2:47 PM Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com> wrote:
From: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, Feb 10, 2026 at 2:47 PM
Subject: Re: Incident bridge template + consistent Jira SUP linking (INT-332 / INT-333)

Aligned on the approach.

On “Waiting on Customer”:
- Agree we should pause *customer-facing* SLA timers when we are blocked, but only if Support clearly documents the specific ask + timestamp in the ticket.
- Internal reminders should continue for P0/P1 (lightweight) to ensure we’re still monitoring impact and ready to re-engage quickly.

Two additional notes for the kickoff template:
1) Add “Customer comms owner” (Support/CS) so we don’t have confusion about who is emailing.
2) Please include a reminder: don’t paste customer-sensitive logs in broadly visible channels; link to the SUP or attach in Jira with correct permissions.

If you incorporate the above, consider this leadership sign-off to proceed with INT-332/INT-333 implementation.

Jasmine

—
Jasmine Liu
VP, Customer Success
Redwood Inference

title: Vertex: Audit log delivery latency — update + mitigation timeline
preview:
From: Rachel Kim <rachel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jasmine Liu <jasmine@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Feb 8, 2026 at 9:14 AM PST
Subject: Vertex: Audit log delivery latency — update + mitigation timeline

Team —

I’m drafting a customer-facing update for Vertex (SUP-1846). Can you sanity-check the root cause wording + confirm the mitigation timeline below is accurate and customer-safe?

Context:
- Vertex is seeing audit log delivery lag >30 min intermittently since Thu.
- They’re asking (1) what changed, (2) whether any events are missing, (3) our ETA + future prevention.

Draft email (customer-facing):
---
Hi Maya,

Thanks again for your patience. We’ve confirmed the cause of the audit log delivery delays you’ve been seeing.

What happened
- Our audit log export pipeline fell behind due to a processing backlog in the delivery workers. The backlog started after a routine change in the pipeline caused a subset of events to retry more aggressively than expected.

Impact
- Audit log delivery was delayed for some customers. We have not seen evidence of dropped audit log events; the issue is latency in delivery (events are eventually delivered once the backlog drains).

What we’re doing now
- We’ve increased processing capacity and are actively draining the backlog.
- We’ve adjusted retry behavior to prevent the same backlog pattern from recurring.

Timeline / next update
- We expect delivery lag to return to normal levels today. We’ll send another update no later than 3:00pm PT, or sooner if the backlog is fully cleared.

Prevention
- We’re adding first-class monitoring/alerting on audit log pipeline lag with on-call thresholds aligned to enterprise expectations.

If you can share 2–3 recent example request IDs and approximate timestamps where you noticed the largest delays, we can cross-check them end-to-end and confirm delivery timing.

Best,
Rachel
---

Please reply with any edits (especially around the “no evidence of dropped events” line and the ETA). If we should avoid mentioning “routine change,” I can generalize.

Thanks,
Rachel

—
Rachel Kim
Enterprise Customer Success Lead
Redwood Inference


From: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rachel Kim <rachel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Feb 8, 2026 at 10:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Vertex: Audit log delivery latency — update + mitigation timeline

Edits/suggestions:

1) Root cause phrasing: I’d avoid “routine change” and “subset of events” unless we’re prepared to explain exactly what. Customer-safe version:
- “A backlog built up in the audit log delivery workers after an internal configuration change increased retry volume.”

2) On dropped events: I’m comfortable saying we have not observed drops in the pipeline, but I’d add a qualifier:
- “We have not observed evidence of dropped events in our pipeline telemetry; we are validating end-to-end against sampled customer events.”

3) ETA: We’re trending down, but I’d avoid a hard “today” promise. Suggest:
- “We expect material improvement over the next several hours and are targeting return to normal delivery latency by end of day PT.”

4) Prevention: mention ENG-4824 without the ID, but keep it concrete:
- “We’re adding a dedicated lag metric + paging thresholds and updating our on-call runbook.”

Otherwise looks good.

— Sean
Sean Gallagher
Site Reliability Engineering


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rachel Kim <rachel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Feb 8, 2026 at 10:18 AM PST
Subject: Re: Vertex: Audit log delivery latency — update + mitigation timeline

+1 to Sean’s edits.

Two additional notes:
- We did a controlled increase in worker concurrency and it’s stable. Backlog is draining at ~2–3x the previous rate.
- We should set expectations that if there’s another spike, we may see short periods of lag while autoscaling catches up (but not 30+ min).

Also, asking for “request IDs” might confuse them since this is audit log delivery. Better ask for:
- example audit log event timestamps + tenant/org id + (if they have it) export job correlation id

— Rafael
Rafael Mendes
SRE (On-call / Escalations)


From: Rachel Kim <rachel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwoodinference.com>

text:
From: Rachel Kim <rachel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jasmine Liu <jasmine@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Feb 8, 2026 at 9:14 AM PST
Subject: Vertex: Audit log delivery latency — update + mitigation timeline

Team —

I’m drafting a customer-facing update for Vertex (SUP-1846). Can you sanity-check the root cause wording + confirm the mitigation timeline below is accurate and customer-safe?

Context:
- Vertex is seeing audit log delivery lag >30 min intermittently since Thu.
- They’re asking (1) what changed, (2) whether any events are missing, (3) our ETA + future prevention.

Draft email (customer-facing):
---
Hi Maya,

Thanks again for your patience. We’ve confirmed the cause of the audit log delivery delays you’ve been seeing.

What happened
- Our audit log export pipeline fell behind due to a processing backlog in the delivery workers. The backlog started after a routine change in the pipeline caused a subset of events to retry more aggressively than expected.

Impact
- Audit log delivery was delayed for some customers. We have not seen evidence of dropped audit log events; the issue is latency in delivery (events are eventually delivered once the backlog drains).

What we’re doing now
- We’ve increased processing capacity and are actively draining the backlog.
- We’ve adjusted retry behavior to prevent the same backlog pattern from recurring.

Timeline / next update
- We expect delivery lag to return to normal levels today. We’ll send another update no later than 3:00pm PT, or sooner if the backlog is fully cleared.

Prevention
- We’re adding first-class monitoring/alerting on audit log pipeline lag with on-call thresholds aligned to enterprise expectations.

If you can share 2–3 recent example request IDs and approximate timestamps where you noticed the largest delays, we can cross-check them end-to-end and confirm delivery timing.

Best,
Rachel
---

Please reply with any edits (especially around the “no evidence of dropped events” line and the ETA). If we should avoid mentioning “routine change,” I can generalize.

Thanks,
Rachel

—
Rachel Kim
Enterprise Customer Success Lead
Redwood Inference


From: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rachel Kim <rachel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Feb 8, 2026 at 10:02 AM PST
Subject: Re: Vertex: Audit log delivery latency — update + mitigation timeline

Edits/suggestions:

1) Root cause phrasing: I’d avoid “routine change” and “subset of events” unless we’re prepared to explain exactly what. Customer-safe version:
- “A backlog built up in the audit log delivery workers after an internal configuration change increased retry volume.”

2) On dropped events: I’m comfortable saying we have not observed drops in the pipeline, but I’d add a qualifier:
- “We have not observed evidence of dropped events in our pipeline telemetry; we are validating end-to-end against sampled customer events.”

3) ETA: We’re trending down, but I’d avoid a hard “today” promise. Suggest:
- “We expect material improvement over the next several hours and are targeting return to normal delivery latency by end of day PT.”

4) Prevention: mention ENG-4824 without the ID, but keep it concrete:
- “We’re adding a dedicated lag metric + paging thresholds and updating our on-call runbook.”

Otherwise looks good.

— Sean
Sean Gallagher
Site Reliability Engineering


From: Rafael Mendes <rafael@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rachel Kim <rachel@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean@redwoodinference.com>, Jasmine Liu <jasmine@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, Feb 8, 2026 at 10:18 AM PST
Subject: Re: Vertex: Audit log delivery latency — update + mitigation timeline

+1 to Sean’s edits.

Two additional notes:
- We did a controlled increase in worker concurrency and it’s stable. Backlog is draining at ~2–3x the previous rate.
- We should set expectations that if there’s another spike, we may see short periods of lag while autoscaling catches up (but not 30+ min).

Also, asking for “request IDs” might confuse them since this is audit log delivery. Better ask for:
- example audit log event timestamps + tenant/org id + (if they have it) export job correlation id

— Rafael
Rafael Mendes
SRE (On-call / Escalations)

title: Exec escalation: Acme AI — streaming timeouts/disconnects (us-east) — approval needed
preview:
From: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 7, 2026 at 7:42 AM PST
Subject: Exec escalation: Acme AI — streaming timeouts/disconnects (us-east) — approval needed
Attachments: Acme_streaming_escalation_summary_2026-02-07.pdf, Acme_customer_email_draft_v2.txt

Team — escalating Acme AI (enterprise) streaming timeouts/disconnects in us-east. They’re requesting (a) an exec-owned written update they can forward internally, and (b) clarity on potential credits given the customer impact.

I need approvals by 10:00am PT on:
1) Customer-facing language (attached draft) — specifically what we can state re: cause + “resolved” wording.
2) Whether we should proactively offer any goodwill / service credits vs. wait for their formal request + post-incident report.

Summary (details in PDF; ticket: SUP-1842):
- Customer: Acme AI (Corp)
- Symptom: SSE streaming responses resetting mid-generation (connection resets / truncated streams). Non-streaming less affected.
- Scope: us-east primarily; intermittent. Most visible on long generations.
- Current status: Mitigated overnight. Still monitoring; no new spike since ~00:10 PT.
- Mitigation: routing adjustments + gateway config rollback; recommended client-side timeout/retry guidance shared with Acme.

Customer asks / context:
- They have an internal exec review Monday and want a statement today.
- They asked for “credits for the incident window” on the call (Rachel has notes).

Proposed customer email language (high-level):
- Acknowledge impact + timeline
- Confirm mitigation applied
- Provide temporary client-side mitigation guidance
- Commit to follow-up RCA within 5 business days
- Avoid promising “fixed permanently” until we confirm longer soak

Please reply-all with:
- “Approved” / edits on the attached customer email
- Credits guidance (ok to offer X%, or wait)

Thanks,
Jasmine

—
Jasmine Liu
VP, Customer Success & Support
Redwood Inference



From: Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 7, 2026 at 8:03 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec escalation: Acme AI — streaming timeouts/disconnects (us-east) — approval needed

Adding SRE technical color for customer-safe wording.

What happened (customer-safe):
- We saw an elevated rate of mid-stream disconnects for streaming requests in us-east, concentrated on longer-lived connections.
- Non-streaming requests had normal success rates; this was primarily a streaming transport stability issue.

Most likely cause (keep high level):
- An interaction between a gateway config change and connection handling under elevated concurrency in us-east led to increased connection resets for long-lived streaming responses.

What we did:
- Rolled back the relevant gateway config and applied a conservative keepalive/connection-handling adjustment.
- Confirmed streaming reset rate returned to baseline in us-east post-mitigation.

Confidence / cautions on wording:
- I’d avoid “root cause fully resolved” in the email. Better: “Mitigation applied; metrics back to baseline; monitoring closely.”
- We can commit to an RCA with preventative follow-ups.

If Acme asks for immediate guidance:
- Recommend setting an explicit client-side read timeout appropriate for their max generation length, and implementing retry with backoff on network disconnects.
- If they can, temporarily switch to HTTP/1.1 for streaming clients behind aggressive proxies (we’ve seen fewer resets).

Rafael



From: Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 7, 2026 at 8:21 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec escalation: Acme AI — streaming timeouts/disconnects (us-east) — approval needed

+1 to Rafael’s guidance.

On the comms draft:
- Approve if we remove/soften any “we identified the root cause” phrasing. I suggest:
  - “We identified a likely contributing factor in gateway connection handling under load and applied a rollback + configuration adjustment.”
  - “Streaming stability metrics have returned to baseline; we’re continuing to monitor.”
- Keep the “RCA within 5 business days” commitment.

On credits:
- Recommend we do not proactively promise credits in the same note as mitigation.
- Suggest language: “If you’d like to request service credits per your agreement, we can evaluate once we finalize the incident timeline and impact assessment.”

Reason: avoids creating a precedent / commitment before we have the full incident accounting and contract interpretation.

Sean



From: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 7, 2026 at 8:47 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec escalation: Acme AI — streaming timeouts/disconnects (us-east) — approval needed

Customer context + what they’re asking for:

- Morgan Lee (Acme Eng) is the point; they looped IT Ops because their internal dashboards lit up as “app instability.” They want a note they can forward to their VP Eng.
- They explicitly asked: “Are we going to get credits? We had a customer-visible incident.”
- They also asked whether they should pin traffic away from us-east for now.

My suggestions for the email:
- Include one sentence recommending temporary routing away from us-east *only if* they can do so without violating their internal requirements. Otherwise, we keep the guidance focused on retries/timeouts.
- Re: credits: agree with Sean — don’t promise. But we should reassure we’ll respond quickly if they submit a request.
- Update cadence: we told them we’d send a written update this morning and another check-in by EOD if anything changes. That’s aligned with what they expect.

Also: please keep wording very explicit that the workaround guidance is temporary and we’ll follow up with long-term prevention.

Rachel



From: Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 7, 2026 at 9:12 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec escalation: Acme AI — streaming timeouts/disconnects (us-east) — approval needed

Adding Sales POV.

Acme is in the middle of expanding usage this quarter. If we go too “legalistic” on credits, we may take a hit in the expansion motion.

Question: are we comfortable with a *goodwill* credit (separate from contractual service credits) once stabilized, e.g., a one-time usage credit applied to next invoice, pending Jasmine approval?

I’m aligned with not committing in writing today. But if Morgan pushes on the call, I’d like a clear internal decision tree:
- If they ask verbally: do we say “we’ll evaluate and get back by X date” (what date)?
- Who signs off (Jasmine vs. Finance)?

Avery



From: Jasmine Liu <jasmine.liu@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwoodinference.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean.gallagher@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwoodinference.com>, Rafael Mendes <rafael.mendes@redwoodinference.com>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sat, Feb 7, 2026 at 10:19 AM PST
Subject: Re: Exec escalation: Acme AI — streaming timeouts/disconnects (us-east) — approval needed

Thanks all — incorporating edits now.

Decision / approvals:
1) Comms language: approved with Sean + Rafael’s edits.
- We will NOT state “root cause confirmed” or “permanently fixed.”
- We WILL state: mitigation applied, streaming stability metrics back to baseline, continued monitoring, RCA to follow.
- We WILL include temporary mitigation guidance (timeouts/retry; optional HTTP/1.1 note) and keep it explicitly “temporary.”

2) Credits:
- We will not proactively offer credits in today’s email.
- Verbal guidance if pressed: “We take this seriously and will evaluate service credits per your agreement once we finalize the incident timeline and impact assessment. We’ll get back to you by Wednesday, Feb 11.”
- Any goodwill credit beyond contractual service credits requires my approval; loop me + Avery.

Rachel: please send the finalized email to Morgan once I drop it into this thread (I’ll forward the final text in ~15 min). Rafael/Sean: thank you — please keep an eye on us-east streaming metrics through the afternoon and post in SUP-1842 if anything moves.

Jasmine

—
Jasmine Liu
VP, Customer Success & Support
Redwood Inference
